dishes.name: Warm Quinoa Bowl with Pan-Seared Chicken, Roasted Veggies & Chickpeas
dishes.description: A balanced, high-protein warm quinoa bowl ready in under 30 minutes: fluffy quinoa, pan-seared lean protein (chicken or tofu), quick-roasted bell pepper, zucchini and red onion, lemony chickpeas, fresh parsley and optional feta. Mild and beginner-friendly.
instructions: Rinse quinoa in a fine mesh strainer. Combine the 0.75 cup quinoa with 1.25 cups water and a pinch of salt in a small saucepan. Bring to a boil, reduce heat, cover and simmer 12–15 minutes until water is absorbed. Remove from heat and let stand covered 5 minutes; fluff with a fork.

While the quinoa cooks, pat the chicken dry and season both sides with salt and pepper. Heat 1 tbsp olive oil in a nonstick skillet over medium-high heat. Cook chicken 4–5 minutes per side (depending on thickness) until internal temperature reaches 165°F (75°C). Remove to a cutting board to rest 3–4 minutes, then slice. (If using tofu: press firm tofu 15 minutes beforehand, cube ~6 oz, and pan-sear 3–4 minutes per side.)

In the same skillet, add the remaining 0.5 tbsp olive oil. Add sliced bell pepper, zucchini and red onion. Sauté 6–8 minutes until vegetables are tender-crisp and slightly browned. Season with a pinch of salt and pepper.
Toss the drained chickpeas with lemon juice and chopped parsley in a small bowl. Add a tiny pinch of salt if desired.

Assemble the bowl: place the cooked quinoa into a bowl, top with sliced chicken (or tofu), sautéed vegetables and lemony chickpeas. Sprinkle with crumbled feta (optional) and extra parsley. Serve warm.

warnings: No allergies specified. Recipes may include dairy, nuts, eggs — flag if you have any intolerances or allergies.
EVAL_TRACE: strict_runs=1, strict_pass=1, llm_runs=1, best_llm_score=95, refinement_loops=0
Contains dairy (feta) — omit to make dairy-free/vegan.
If using chicken, ensure internal temperature reaches 165°F (75°C). If substituting tofu, use ~6 oz pressed tofu for comparable texture; protein will change slightly.
suggestions: Research suggests prioritizing lunch as the principal meal supports metabolic efficiency for evening chronotypes; this lunch is protein-forward and vegetable-rich.
Batch-cook quinoa and roast extra vegetables ahead (e.g., Sunday) to keep weekday prep under 30 minutes.
Include canned legumes (chickpeas) for quick fiber and plant protein.

This recipe was evaluated as written; ingredient quantities represent the full recipe used to produce the stated nutrition_per_serving. If you want two smaller servings, split as preferred but nutrition_per_serving will change.
If quinoa seems undercooked, add up to 2–3 tbsp extra water during cooking and check for tenderness; a 1.25 cups water ratio works for many stovetops but may vary.

To lower calories/fat, reduce olive oil to 1 tbsp total and skip or reduce feta; to boost protein (still under max 40 g), increase chicken to 6 oz or add 1–2 tbsp Greek yogurt as a sauce (if dairy is acceptable).

dishes.name: Tuna & White-Bean Salad with Mixed Greens
dishes.description: A quick no-cook, protein-forward lunch: canned tuna and cannellini beans tossed with fresh vegetables, lemon and olive oil, served over mixed greens with a slice of whole-grain bread.
instructions: Drain the tuna and cannellini beans well. Place the tuna in a medium bowl and flake lightly with a fork.
Add the drained cannellini beans to the bowl and gently mash about one-third of the beans with the fork to create a creamier texture while leaving the rest whole.
Stir in chopped celery, halved cherry tomatoes, finely chopped red onion, and parsley.
In a small bowl or jar, whisk together the lemon juice and olive oil with a pinch of salt and black pepper (to taste).
Pour the dressing over the tuna-and-bean mixture and toss gently to combine.
Arrange mixed greens on a plate, top with the tuna-and-bean mixture, and serve with the slice of whole-grain bread on the side.

warnings: Canned fish and legumes are used; check freshness and sodium levels if you are monitoring salt intake.
EVAL_TRACE: strict_runs=1, strict_pass=1, llm_runs=1, best_llm_score=94, refinement_loops=0
Nutrition estimates (notably fiber) are somewhat optimistic for the portion; canned ingredients can be higher in sodium—use low-sodium cans or rinse/drain to reduce sodium.
suggestions: Using canned, ready-to-eat proteins and beans keeps prep time low and supports adequate protein intake for muscle maintenance.
Research suggests Mediterranean-style lunches (vegetables + legumes + lean protein) help metabolic health—this meal follows that pattern.
Use low-sodium canned tuna and beans or rinse beans thoroughly to lower sodium; optionally reduce olive oil to 10 g to cut fat/calories.
For reliably higher fiber, increase the beans by ~30 g or add extra vegetables (e.g., bell pepper) or a larger slice of whole-grain bread.
If gluten-free is needed, swap the bread for a gluten-free roll or omit it; seasoning (salt/pepper) is optional to taste.

dishes.name: Warm Lentil & Roasted Sweet Potato Salad with Tahini-Lemon Dressing
dishes.description: A quick, plant-forward warm main: tender brown lentils and roasted sweet potato tossed with spinach (or arugula), toasted sunflower seeds and a simple tahini-lemon dressing. Beginner-friendly and ready in ~30 minutes.
instructions: Preheat oven to 425°F (220°C). Toss diced sweet potato with 1/2 tbsp olive oil and a pinch of salt and pepper; spread in a single layer on a baking sheet.
Roast sweet potato for 18–22 minutes, turning once, until edges are browned and pieces are tender.

While sweet potato roasts, rinse lentils. Place lentils in a pot with 3 cups (750 ml) water, bring to a boil, reduce to a simmer and cook uncovered 18–20 minutes until tender but holding shape. Drain any excess water.
Make the dressing: whisk together tahini, lemon juice, remaining 1/2 tbsp olive oil, minced garlic and 1/4 tsp salt. If too thick, thin with 1–2 tsp warm water until pourable.
In a large bowl combine warm lentils, roasted sweet potato, greens and sunflower seeds. Pour dressing over and gently toss to combine. Taste and adjust salt and pepper.
Divide between plates (recipe yields ~2 servings). If using, crumble feta on top of each serving. Serve warm or at room temperature.

warnings: Contains nuts/seeds and possibly dairy if cheese is added; remove or replace if you have sensitivities.
EVAL_TRACE: strict_runs=2, strict_pass=2, llm_runs=2, best_llm_score=94, refinement_loops=1
Contains tahini (sesame) and sunflower seeds — common allergens. Feta is optional (dairy) — omit or substitute for vegan/dairy-free.
Total time equals the user's available 30 minutes; this requires multitasking (start lentils and roast sweet potato together).
suggestions: Lentils and sweet potato provide slow-release carbs and fiber to support daytime energy without heavy evening digestion.
Prepare lentils in a larger batch for 2–3 meals to save time.
To save time: use 1 can (~400 g) cooked lentils (drained) — skip simmering and shorten total time.
To lower calories: reduce tahini to 1 tbsp and use 1 tsp olive oil or omit feta.
Vegan option: omit feta and add 2 tbsp extra toasted seeds or 1/4 cup cooked quinoa for extra protein.
Start lentils and roast simultaneously; stir/turn sweet potato once to stay within 30 min. Pre-cook lentils ahead for faster assembly.

dishes.name: Chicken Caesar Wrap
dishes.description: Quick 15-minute whole-wheat wrap with cooked chicken (or swap turkey), romaine, tomato and a sprinkle of Parmesan — handheld lunch, easy to assemble.

ingredients.name: boneless skinless chicken breast (or thinly sliced deli turkey)

instructions: If using raw chicken, thinly slice and quickly pan-sear for 4–5 minutes until cooked through; for fastest assembly use pre-cooked chicken or deli turkey slices.
Lay the whole wheat tortilla flat. Layer romaine, tomato slices, cooked chicken/turkey, and sprinkle Parmesan.
Optionally fold in a light Caesar-style dressing (use sparingly to control calories) or a smear of hummus for Mediterranean twist.
Roll tightly, cut in half if desired, and serve with carrot sticks or a small side salad.

warnings: Pre-sliced deli meats can be higher in sodium and preservatives; choose minimally processed options where possible.
EVAL_TRACE: strict_runs=1, strict_pass=1, llm_runs=1, best_llm_score=90, refinement_loops=0
Fiber is slightly below the requested minimum (4.2 g vs 5 g). Dressing (if added) may increase calories/fat and introduce allergens (dairy/eggs) via classic Caesar dressing.
suggestions: Handheld wraps are quick to assemble and portable for student schedules—keep hummus and pre-washed greens on hand.
If aiming to increase muscle gain, add a dairy or plant-protein side (e.g., yogurt or a protein shake) after this lunch or post-workout.
Add a side of carrot sticks, an apple, or extra shredded cabbage/romaine to raise fiber to meet the >=5 g target.

Swap the chicken for thinly sliced roast turkey (matches your description) or use smashed chickpeas + a touch of lemon and cumin for a vegetarian option; chickpea version will lower protein unless you increase portion size.
To guarantee the 15-minute window, use pre-cooked rotisserie chicken or deli turkey slices and pre-shredded lettuce.

dishes.name: Greek Salmon Brown Rice Bowl
dishes.description: Quick Greek-style bowl with a base of cooked brown rice, pan-seared salmon, a simple cucumber-tomato salad with Kalamata olives and oregano, plus a light tzatziki-style yogurt scoop. High-protein and fiber-rich, beginner-friendly and ready in ~25 minutes when rice is pre-cooked or using quick-cook rice.
instructions: If using pre-cooked or microwaveable brown rice, heat according to package while you prepare the rest (keeps total time ~25 minutes). If starting from dry brown rice, cook separately (may push total time >30 min).
Pat salmon dry and season both sides with a pinch (about 1/8 tsp) of salt and a little black pepper.

Heat a nonstick skillet over medium-high. Add 1 tsp of the olive oil to the pan. When hot, place the salmon skin-side down (if present). Sear without moving 3–4 minutes to crisp, then flip and cook 3–4 more minutes until opaque and it flakes easily with a fork (or reaches 145°F / 63°C internal temperature). Remove and let rest.

While the salmon cooks, make the cucumber-tomato salad: dice the cucumber and tomato and place in a bowl. Add the olives (halved if preferred), 1 tsp olive oil, 1/2 tbsp (1.5 tsp) lemon juice, 1/4 tsp dried oregano, and a pinch of salt and pepper. Toss to combine.

Make quick tzatziki: in a small bowl, mix Greek yogurt with minced garlic, the remaining 1/2 tbsp (1.5 tsp) lemon juice, a pinch of dried oregano, and a little black pepper. Stir and taste; adjust lemon or garlic as desired.

Assemble the bowl: place 1 cup warm brown rice in a bowl, top with the salmon fillet, spoon the cucumber-tomato salad to one side, and add a scoop (about 1/3 cup) of the yogurt tzatziki on the side. Finish with a squeeze of lemon over the salmon if desired.
Optional garnish: extra oregano or cracked black pepper. For a vegetarian swap, replace salmon with grilled marinated tempeh (see suggestions).

warnings: Seafood and dairy present in this meal; omit or swap if you have sensitivities or preferences.
EVAL_TRACE: strict_runs=1, strict_pass=1, llm_runs=1, best_llm_score=93, refinement_loops=0
If you start from dry brown rice (not pre-cooked or quick-cook), total time will likely exceed 30 minutes.
Sodium note: Kalamata olives and added salt raise sodium—reduce olives or rinse them to lower sodium if needed.
suggestions: Including a higher-protein lunch before an evening workout is useful for overall daily protein distribution supporting muscle gain.
Research suggests Mediterranean-style components (vegetables, legumes, lean protein) improve metabolic markers—this bowl follows that approach.
To keep within 30 minutes, use microwaveable pre-cooked brown rice or quick-cook farro (check package cook time).
Low-sodium options: halve the olives or rinse them, reduce added salt, or substitute low-sodium olives.
Doneness tip: cook salmon to 145°F / 63°C internal temp or until flesh is opaque and flakes easily; thicker fillets may need an extra minute or two per side.
Vegetarian swap: grill or pan-sear 4–5 oz tempeh slices marinated 10–15 minutes in lemon, garlic, olive oil, and oregano — this will change nutrition values (protein may vary).
Make extra rice ahead and refrigerate portions to cut prep time on busy days.

fitness.goal: Build full-body strength and hypertrophy using progressive bodyweight variations and time-under-tension.

items: 3 minutes light cardio in place (march or easy jog)
Dynamic mobility: leg swings, arm circles, hip circles (3 min)
2 rounds: 10 slow bodyweight squats + 6 slow incline or knee push-ups
A. Push: Push-ups 4 sets, 8-12 reps, 90s rest
B. Legs: Bodyweight squats (or slow tempo squats) 4 sets, 12-20 reps, 60s rest
C. Unilateral: Reverse lunges 3 sets, 8-12 each leg, 75s rest
D. Hinge: Glute bridge (progress to single-leg) 3 sets, 12-20 reps, 45s rest
E. Core/upper-back: Planks 3 sets, 45-60s (or 3 x 12 supermans), 45s rest
Standing hamstring & quad stretch, chest opener (30-45s each)
Controlled breathing and gentle shoulder mobility

exercise_details.name: Push-ups
exercise_details.notes: 2s descent, 1s ascent. Regress to knee or incline push-ups if needed. If >12 reps easy, use slower eccentrics (3s down) or pause at bottom.

exercise_details.name: Bodyweight Squats
exercise_details.notes: Full depth as comfortable. Slow negatives increase difficulty. If too easy, try narrow/tempo variations or pause at the bottom.

exercise_details.name: Reverse Lunges
exercise_details.notes: Step back into the lunge to reduce knee stress. Keep torso tall. If balance is an issue, hold a wall or chair for light support.

exercise_details.name: Glute Bridge
exercise_details.notes: Squeeze glutes at the top for 2s. Progress to single-leg glute bridge when 15-20 reps become easy.

exercise_details.name: Plank or Supermans
exercise_details.notes: Choose plank for core endurance or supermans for posterior chain. Maintain a neutral spine and avoid sagging hips.

safety_notes: Estimated timing: warmup ~10 min; main ~45 min (including rests); cooldown ~5 min. Use a timer for rests.
Form-first: stop any movement that causes sharp joint pain. Differentiate muscle burn from joint pain by reducing range or regressing movement.
Progression: add 1 rep per set every 1–2 weeks; when upper bound is sustainable for two sessions, add a set or increase difficulty (tempo, unilateral variations).

draft.goal: Develop lower-body strength, unilateral control, and metabolic conditioning to support hypertrophy and work capacity.

items: 5 minutes light cardio (marching, high knees, or bike if at gym)
Dynamic leg mobility: hip circles, leg swings, ankle mobility
2 sets: 8 slow bodyweight squats + 6 forward or reverse lunges each side
A. Single-leg hinge: Single-leg Romanian deadlift (bodyweight reach) 3 sets, 10 each side, 60s rest
B. Quad/glute: Walking lunges or reverse lunges 4 sets, 12-16 steps each leg, 60s rest
C. Power/conditioning: Jump squats or squat pulses 3 sets, 8-12 reps, 90s rest
D. Posterior chain: Single-leg glute bridge 3 sets, 10-12 each leg, 45s rest
E. Core & calves superset: Standing calf raises 3 x 20 + Dead-bug 3 x 12, 30-45s rest between supersets
Self-massage or foam-roll substitute for quads & hamstrings (2-3 min each)
Static stretches for hips, hamstrings, calves (30-45s each)

exercise_details.name: Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift (bodyweight reach)
exercise_details.notes: Hinge at hips, reach toward the ground with opposite hand for balance. Slight bend in standing knee. Use a chair for support if needed.

exercise_details.name: Walking Lunges / Reverse Lunges
exercise_details.notes: Long stride emphasizes glutes; keep torso tall and control descent. If space is limited, do reverse lunges in place.

exercise_details.name: Jump Squats or Squat Pulses
exercise_details.notes: If joint-sensitive, prefer squat pulses. For jumps, land softly and absorb force through hips and knees.

exercise_details.name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge
exercise_details.notes: Drive through the heel and squeeze glutes at the top. Add a 2s pause at top for extra tension.

exercise_details.name: Standing Calf Raises + Dead Bug
exercise_details.notes: Perform as a superset to save time: calf raises immediately followed by dead bug. Keep core engaged during dead bug.

safety_notes: Estimated timing: warmup ~10, main ~40, cooldown ~10. If short on time, superset compatible movements and reduce rest to 45s.
When performing jump variations, ensure a clear, slightly cushioned landing surface to protect joints.
Balance regressions: hold a stable surface (chair/wall) during single-leg work until stable.
Progression: +1–2 reps per set every 1–2 weeks. Once you hit the upper bound across sets for two sessions, add one set or increase difficulty (tempo, pauses, or add load at the gym).

history.response_text: Done — I created a 3-session weekly evening workout plan (Mon/Wed/Fri at ~19:00) focused on muscle gain with no-special-equipment primary options, plus a simple 30-minute lunch prep plan for Monday–Friday (default lunch time 12:00). draft.goal: Prioritize upper-body pushing and posterior-chain (pull) strength with safe no-equipment pulling alternatives; core stability finish.

items: Arm swings, shoulder circles, thoracic rotations (5 minutes)
2 sets: 8 incline or regular push-ups + 10 scapular push-ups
A. Horizontal push: Push-up variations 4 sets, 8-12 reps, 90s rest
B. Pull/posterior alternative: High-rep supermans + scapular retraction drills 4 sets, 10-15 reps, 75s rest
C. Vertical push: Pike push-ups 3 sets, 6-10 reps, 90s rest
D. Triceps: Chair dips or bench-supported dips 3 sets, 8-15 reps, 60s rest
E. Core circuit: 3 rounds — Side plank 30-45s each side + Hollow-body rocks x12, 45s rest
Doorway chest stretch, posterior shoulder stretch, child's pose (30-45s each)
Controlled breathing to return heart rate to baseline

exercise_details.name: Push-up variations (standard/incline/decline as suitable)
exercise_details.notes: Select a variation that challenges you in the target range. Slow eccentrics increase difficulty without equipment.

exercise_details.name: Supermans + Scapular Retractions (posterior alternative)
exercise_details.notes: Lie prone for supermans (hold 2s at top). Follow with scapular retraction drills (standing or prone). These are the primary no-equipment pulling substitutes. If you have safe gym anchors, consider inverted rows or cable rows as progressions.

exercise_details.name: Pike Push-ups
exercise_details.notes: Progress by elevating feet for more range; regress by using a higher incline. Keep neck neutral.

exercise_details.name: Chair Dips
exercise_details.notes: Use a sturdy chair. Keep shoulders down and stop if anterior shoulder pain occurs. Regress with bench-supported push-ups or triceps extensions (if gym weights become available).

exercise_details.name: Side Plank + Hollow-Body Rocks
exercise_details.reps: Side plank: 30-45s each side / Hollow rocks: 12
exercise_details.notes: Maintain ribcage tucked and neutral pelvis. Short high-quality holds are preferable to long collapsing holds.

safety_notes: Primary pulling options are bodyweight posterior alternatives (supermans, scapular retractions). Only use household anchors (table/door towel rows) after a careful safety check of the anchor — stop immediately if any wobble/creak is felt.
Optional gym progressions: inverted rows, assisted pull-ups, lat pulldown, or single-arm rows. Only add these if you have access to appropriate gym equipment and know safe setup.
Slow controlled tempo is better than many fast reps. If you experience sharp joint pain, stop and regress the exercise.

dishes.name: Quick Grilled Chicken & Quinoa Bowl (Yogurt-Herb Dressing)
dishes.description: Thin-sliced, quick-pan-seared chicken served over cooked quinoa with chickpeas, cucumber, cherry tomatoes and mixed greens. Simple lemon-yogurt herb dressing keeps fat moderate while delivering high protein and fiber. Designed for ≤30 minutes using quick-cook or pre-cooked quinoa.
instructions: Prep: Pat the chicken dry and season both sides with salt and pepper. If thick, slice horizontally or pound to ~1/2" thickness so it cooks quickly.

Heat 1 tsp olive oil in a nonstick skillet over medium-high heat. Add chicken and cook 3–4 minutes per side until golden and internal temperature reaches 165°F (74°C). Remove and let rest 2–3 minutes, then slice thinly.
Reheat 1 cup cooked quinoa per package instructions (or cook quick-cook quinoa) so it is ready within the time limit.
Make dressing: whisk together Greek yogurt, tahini, lemon juice, minced garlic and a pinch of salt; add 1–2 tsp water to thin if needed.
Assemble bowl: place quinoa in bowl, top with mixed greens, cucumber, cherry tomatoes, chickpeas and sliced chicken. Drizzle dressing and serve.

warnings: Contains poultry — cook to safe internal temperature (165°F / 74°C) and avoid cross-contamination.
If adding tahini or yogurt, note these are common allergens for some people (user currently reports no allergies).

suggestions: Research suggests distributing protein across meals supports muscle maintenance and growth; aim for ~30+ g protein at this meal.
To save time, cook a larger batch of quinoa and chicken earlier in the week for quick assembly.
Include a small high-protein snack (e.g., Greek yogurt or a protein shake) after gym sessions if additional protein is needed.
Reduce tahini to 1/2 tsp or use nonfat Greek yogurt to lower fat/calories slightly.
Thin-slice chicken before cooking (or pound) to ensure 3–4 min per side timing for beginners.
Make extra quinoa ahead to save time on future meals and ensure consistent nutrition targets.

dishes.name: Pan-Seared Salmon with Mashed Sweet Potato and Sautéed Spinach
dishes.description: Beginner-friendly pan-seared salmon served with mashed sweet potato and quickly sautéed spinach. Emphasizes lean protein and omega-3s; ready in ~35 minutes.
instructions: Preheat oven to 220°C (425°F) if roasting sweet potato; otherwise prepare a microwave-safe dish. Toss cubed sweet potato with a pinch of salt and roast 22–25 minutes until tender and lightly browned, or microwave 6–8 minutes (turn once) until soft.
While sweet potato cooks, pat salmon dry and season both sides with about 0.25 tsp salt and a pinch of pepper.

Heat a nonstick or heavy skillet over medium-high heat with ~2 tsp of the olive oil. When oil is hot and shimmering, add salmon skin-side down (if skin on). Sear without moving 3–4 minutes until a golden crust forms. Flip and cook 2–3 minutes more for medium; add 1–2 minutes if thicker or you prefer well done. (Cook until fish flakes easily or reaches 63°C/145°F.) Remove salmon and squeeze half the lemon over the fillet.

Lower heat to medium, add remaining oil as needed and sauté minced garlic 30 seconds until fragrant. Add spinach (or kale) and toss until wilted and bright green — 1–2 minutes for spinach (2–3 minutes for kale). Season with remaining salt, pepper and a squeeze of lemon.

When sweet potato is tender, drain and mash with a fork or masher. Season with a pinch of salt and a little lemon zest or pepper. For a creamier mash, stir in up to 1 tsp olive oil or a tablespoon of warm water.
Plate: spoon mashed sweet potato, place salmon on top or beside it, add sautéed greens, and garnish with the remaining lemon wedge.

dishes.name: Microwaved Sweet Potato Mash
dishes.description: Quick mashed sweet potato prepared in the microwave: a simple source of complex carbs and potassium, lightly seasoned with olive oil, salt, and pepper. Beginner-friendly, ready in ~20 minutes.
instructions: Pierce the sweet potato several times all over with a fork.
Place on a microwave-safe plate and microwave on high for 8–12 minutes, turning once halfway, until very tender (see wattage note in suggestions).
Slice open and scoop the flesh into a bowl.
Mash with olive oil and optional butter until smooth; season with salt and black pepper to taste.
Serve warm alongside the pan-seared salmon and sautéed greens.

dishes.name: Quick Sautéed Spinach with Garlic, Lemon & Toasted Pumpkin Seeds
dishes.description: A fast, nutrient-dense side of wilted spinach brightened with lemon and finished with a small handful of toasted pumpkin seeds for texture, extra protein and healthy fats. Beginner-friendly and ready in under 15 minutes — pairs perfectly with pan-seared salmon and sweet potato.
instructions: Heat a large nonstick skillet over medium-high heat. Add the olive oil and swirl to coat.
Add the minced garlic and sauté 30 seconds until fragrant, taking care not to brown it.
Add the spinach (in batches if needed) and toss/sauté until just wilted, about 2–4 minutes total.
Remove from heat, stir in lemon juice and salt, sprinkle toasted pumpkin seeds on top, and serve immediately.

warnings: Contains fish (salmon) — check for freshness and cook to a safe internal temperature. If allergic to fish, substitute with tofu or lean poultry.
If adding nuts/seeds, these are common allergens—omit if needed.

suggestions: Research suggests favoring lean proteins and vegetables in the evening and including tryptophan-rich foods (e.g., salmon, dairy, nuts) may support sleep quality.

Microwave timing guide: ~8–10 min for 1000 W, ~10–12 min for 700–800 W; test doneness with a fork.
This recipe yields approximately 1 side serving (nutrition per serving as shown). Double quantities for additional servings.

If using kale instead of spinach, increase cook time by 2–3 minutes and add a splash of water to help soften the leaves.

dishes.name: Warm Quinoa Bowl with Pan-Seared Chicken, Roasted Veggies & Chickpeas
dishes.description: A balanced, high-protein warm quinoa bowl ready in under 30 minutes: fluffy quinoa, pan-seared lean protein (chicken or tofu), quick-roasted bell pepper, zucchini and red onion, lemony chickpeas, fresh parsley and optional feta. Mild and beginner-friendly.
instructions: Rinse quinoa in a fine mesh strainer. Combine the 0.75 cup quinoa with 1.25 cups water and a pinch of salt in a small saucepan. Bring to a boil, reduce heat, cover and simmer 12–15 minutes until water is absorbed. Remove from heat and let stand covered 5 minutes; fluff with a fork.

While the quinoa cooks, pat the chicken dry and season both sides with salt and pepper. Heat 1 tbsp olive oil in a nonstick skillet over medium-high heat. Cook chicken 4–5 minutes per side (depending on thickness) until internal temperature reaches 165°F (75°C). Remove to a cutting board to rest 3–4 minutes, then slice. (If using tofu: press firm tofu 15 minutes beforehand, cube ~6 oz, and pan-sear 3–4 minutes per side.)

In the same skillet, add the remaining 0.5 tbsp olive oil. Add sliced bell pepper, zucchini and red onion. Sauté 6–8 minutes until vegetables are tender-crisp and slightly browned. Season with a pinch of salt and pepper.
Toss the drained chickpeas with lemon juice and chopped parsley in a small bowl. Add a tiny pinch of salt if desired.

Assemble the bowl: place the cooked quinoa into a bowl, top with sliced chicken (or tofu), sautéed vegetables and lemony chickpeas. Sprinkle with crumbled feta (optional) and extra parsley. Serve warm.

warnings: No allergies specified. Recipes may include dairy, nuts, eggs — flag if you have any intolerances or allergies.

suggestions: Research suggests prioritizing lunch as the principal meal supports metabolic efficiency for evening chronotypes; this lunch is protein-forward and vegetable-rich.
Batch-cook quinoa and roast extra vegetables ahead (e.g., Sunday) to keep weekday prep under 30 minutes.
Include canned legumes (chickpeas) for quick fiber and plant protein.

This recipe was evaluated as written; ingredient quantities represent the full recipe used to produce the stated nutrition_per_serving. If you want two smaller servings, split as preferred but nutrition_per_serving will change.
If quinoa seems undercooked, add up to 2–3 tbsp extra water during cooking and check for tenderness; a 1.25 cups water ratio works for many stovetops but may vary.

To lower calories/fat, reduce olive oil to 1 tbsp total and skip or reduce feta; to boost protein (still under max 40 g), increase chicken to 6 oz or add 1–2 tbsp Greek yogurt as a sauce (if dairy is acceptable).

dishes.name: Tuna & White-Bean Salad with Mixed Greens
dishes.description: A quick no-cook, protein-forward lunch: canned tuna and cannellini beans tossed with fresh vegetables, lemon and olive oil, served over mixed greens with a slice of whole-grain bread.
instructions: Drain the tuna and cannellini beans well. Place the tuna in a medium bowl and flake lightly with a fork.
Add the drained cannellini beans to the bowl and gently mash about one-third of the beans with the fork to create a creamier texture while leaving the rest whole.
Stir in chopped celery, halved cherry tomatoes, finely chopped red onion, and parsley.
In a small bowl or jar, whisk together the lemon juice and olive oil with a pinch of salt and black pepper (to taste).
Pour the dressing over the tuna-and-bean mixture and toss gently to combine.
Arrange mixed greens on a plate, top with the tuna-and-bean mixture, and serve with the slice of whole-grain bread on the side.

warnings: Canned fish and legumes are used; check freshness and sodium levels if you are monitoring salt intake.

For reliably higher fiber, increase the beans by ~30 g or add extra vegetables (e.g., bell pepper) or a larger slice of whole-grain bread.
If gluten-free is needed, swap the bread for a gluten-free roll or omit it; seasoning (salt/pepper) is optional to taste.

dishes.name: Warm Lentil & Roasted Sweet Potato Salad with Tahini-Lemon Dressing
dishes.description: A quick, plant-forward warm main: tender brown lentils and roasted sweet potato tossed with spinach (or arugula), toasted sunflower seeds and a simple tahini-lemon dressing. Beginner-friendly and ready in ~30 minutes.
instructions: Preheat oven to 425°F (220°C). Toss diced sweet potato with 1/2 tbsp olive oil and a pinch of salt and pepper; spread in a single layer on a baking sheet.
Roast sweet potato for 18–22 minutes, turning once, until edges are browned and pieces are tender.

While sweet potato roasts, rinse lentils. Place lentils in a pot with 3 cups (750 ml) water, bring to a boil, reduce to a simmer and cook uncovered 18–20 minutes until tender but holding shape. Drain any excess water.
Make the dressing: whisk together tahini, lemon juice, remaining 1/2 tbsp olive oil, minced garlic and 1/4 tsp salt. If too thick, thin with 1–2 tsp warm water until pourable.
In a large bowl combine warm lentils, roasted sweet potato, greens and sunflower seeds. Pour dressing over and gently toss to combine. Taste and adjust salt and pepper.
Divide between plates (recipe yields ~2 servings). If using, crumble feta on top of each serving. Serve warm or at room temperature.

warnings: Contains nuts/seeds and possibly dairy if cheese is added; remove or replace if you have sensitivities.

Prepare lentils in a larger batch for 2–3 meals to save time.

dishes.name: Chicken Caesar Wrap
dishes.description: Quick 15-minute whole-wheat wrap with cooked chicken (or swap turkey), romaine, tomato and a sprinkle of Parmesan — handheld lunch, easy to assemble.

ingredients.name: boneless skinless chicken breast (or thinly sliced deli turkey)

instructions: If using raw chicken, thinly slice and quickly pan-sear for 4–5 minutes until cooked through; for fastest assembly use pre-cooked chicken or deli turkey slices.
Lay the whole wheat tortilla flat. Layer romaine, tomato slices, cooked chicken/turkey, and sprinkle Parmesan.
Optionally fold in a light Caesar-style dressing (use sparingly to control calories) or a smear of hummus for Mediterranean twist.
Roll tightly, cut in half if desired, and serve with carrot sticks or a small side salad.

warnings: Pre-sliced deli meats can be higher in sodium and preservatives; choose minimally processed options where possible.

dishes.name: Greek Salmon Brown Rice Bowl
dishes.description: Quick Greek-style bowl with a base of cooked brown rice, pan-seared salmon, a simple cucumber-tomato salad with Kalamata olives and oregano, plus a light tzatziki-style yogurt scoop. High-protein and fiber-rich, beginner-friendly and ready in ~25 minutes when rice is pre-cooked or using quick-cook rice.
instructions: If using pre-cooked or microwaveable brown rice, heat according to package while you prepare the rest (keeps total time ~25 minutes). If starting from dry brown rice, cook separately (may push total time >30 min).
Pat salmon dry and season both sides with a pinch (about 1/8 tsp) of salt and a little black pepper.

Heat a nonstick skillet over medium-high. Add 1 tsp of the olive oil to the pan. When hot, place the salmon skin-side down (if present). Sear without moving 3–4 minutes to crisp, then flip and cook 3–4 more minutes until opaque and it flakes easily with a fork (or reaches 145°F / 63°C internal temperature). Remove and let rest.

While the salmon cooks, make the cucumber-tomato salad: dice the cucumber and tomato and place in a bowl. Add the olives (halved if preferred), 1 tsp olive oil, 1/2 tbsp (1.5 tsp) lemon juice, 1/4 tsp dried oregano, and a pinch of salt and pepper. Toss to combine.

Make quick tzatziki: in a small bowl, mix Greek yogurt with minced garlic, the remaining 1/2 tbsp (1.5 tsp) lemon juice, a pinch of dried oregano, and a little black pepper. Stir and taste; adjust lemon or garlic as desired.

Assemble the bowl: place 1 cup warm brown rice in a bowl, top with the salmon fillet, spoon the cucumber-tomato salad to one side, and add a scoop (about 1/3 cup) of the yogurt tzatziki on the side. Finish with a squeeze of lemon over the salmon if desired.
Optional garnish: extra oregano or cracked black pepper. For a vegetarian swap, replace salmon with grilled marinated tempeh (see suggestions).

warnings: Seafood and dairy present in this meal; omit or swap if you have sensitivities or preferences.
EVAL_TRACE: strict_runs=1, strict_pass=1, llm_runs=1, best_llm_score=93, refinement_loops=0
If you start from dry brown rice (not pre-cooked or quick-cook), total time will likely exceed 30 minutes.
Sodium note: Kalamata olives and added salt raise sodium—reduce olives or rinse them to lower sodium if needed.
suggestions: Including a higher-protein lunch before an evening workout is useful for overall daily protein distribution supporting muscle gain.
Research suggests Mediterranean-style components (vegetables, legumes, lean protein) improve metabolic markers—this bowl follows that approach.
To keep within 30 minutes, use microwaveable pre-cooked brown rice or quick-cook farro (check package cook time).
Low-sodium options: halve the olives or rinse them, reduce added salt, or substitute low-sodium olives.
Doneness tip: cook salmon to 145°F / 63°C internal temp or until flesh is opaque and flakes easily; thicker fillets may need an extra minute or two per side.
Vegetarian swap: grill or pan-sear 4–5 oz tempeh slices marinated 10–15 minutes in lemon, garlic, olive oil, and oregano — this will change nutrition values (protein may vary).
Make extra rice ahead and refrigerate portions to cut prep time on busy days.
